entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: How to train SAP Joule to act as your weatherman
entry.content:
Friday afternoon fun with SAP Joule, can I make Joule share weather conditions? In order to do so I need three things:

  1. SAP BTP with Joule activated
  2. A weather API
  3. A new Joule skill

In the video below I share a step by step approach to get your weather conditions in SAP Joule.

  • An SAP BTP destination to the weather API
  • An SAP Build Action to call the API
  • An SAP Joule Skill

Next is to get a proposal on what to wear based on weather conditions 😀

entry.title: Automating SAP HANA Cloud (Other Environment) DB Lifecycle Management with SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
In our recent updates around AI-enabled Ops Operations, we demonstrated how SAP Automation Pilot boosts structured, scalable, and intelligent automation for Day-2 Ops Agents through Joule.

Today, I am excited to take this one step further.

🚀  We are expanding our automation coverage to fully address SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle management in the Other Environment - bringing operational excellence to database management at scale.

From Standard Catalogs to Broader Lifecycle Automation

SAP Automation Pilot already provides a built-in catalog for managing SAP HANA Cloud database instances running in the Cloud Foundry environment - that is the the well-known SAP BTP Database Lifecycle Management (dblm-sapcp) Catalog.

This catalog has successfully helped customers automate essential lifecycle tasks directly within SAP BTP.

However, many customers also operate SAP HANA Cloud instances in the Other Environment, where lifecycle management is equally critical and operationally sensitive. While SAP Automation Pilot already offers all the technical capabilities required to automate those tasks, we recognized the need to package common scenarios into a structured, reusable, and publicly available catalog.

That is exactly what we have now delivered! 

Introducing: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

We have consolidated a wide range of SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle scenarios into a dedicated public sample catalog, now available in our GitHub repository here: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

This catalog provides a comprehensive and validated set of automation commands for managing SAP HANA Cloud instances at subaccount level using Service Manager.

Instead of executing repetitive and sensitive database operations manually, you can now automate domains such as: 🔹 Instance Lifecycle Management 🔹Backup & Recovery 🔹 Snapshot Management 🔹 Instance Configuration🔹 Upgrades & Plugins 🔹 Elastic Compute Nodes (ECN), etc. 

✅ All commands are validated and ready to be explored by our users - to help you minimize manual effort, reduce risk, and standardize operational procedures.

Beyond Automation: Toward Intelligent Database Operations

While structured lifecycle automation already delivers immediate value, the real transformation begins when automation becomes agentic.

Because all HANA Cloud-related commands are exposed through SAP Automation Pilot, they can be orchestrated, combined, and consumed in higher-level operational workflows. 

This naturally leads to the next evolution - AI Ops Agents for HANA Cloud enabled with SAP Automation Pilot and Joule! 

📖Be Part of the Journey - Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative

As we continue expanding agentic AI operations, collaboration with customers remains key.

If you would like to gain hands-on experience with AI-enabled Ops automation and help shape the future of intelligent database operations, we invite you to subscribe in our ongoing Customer Engagement Initiative: Agentic AI Operations with SAP Automation Pilot

Through this initiative, you can:

  • Explore real-world AI-driven automation scenarios;
  • Bring your own use cases and operational challenges;
  • Provide direct feedback to the product team;
  • Influence upcoming capabilities;

⏳ Registration is open until March 15, 2026 - secure your spot soon!

From structured lifecycle automation to fully agentic database operations - this is the next chapter of operational excellence with SAP Automation Pilot.

Let’s build it together!

entry.title: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI) on https://influence.sap.com allow you, as an SAP customer or partner, to gain early insights into SAP's product developments and work directly with SAP teams to shape future product directions. This project presents a unique opportunity to work with SAPUI5 flexibility product managers and engineering teams, leveraging your expertise customizing SAPUI5 apps with key user adaptation.

Join the upcoming CEI project to make an impact on how key users interact with key user adaptation

If you’re already convinced – join here. If not, read on 🙂

What is key user adaptation?

Key user adaptation is a mode that allows extending SAPUI5 applications for other users right in the browser. It is highly intuitive and requires no technical or programming skills. Just use drag and drop to make UI changes – no modification or copy of SAP standard coding necessary! Read more here.

What is the targeted improvement?

Although the product is mature and widely used, we actively evolve it by delivering new functionality and capability enhancements alongside continuous user experience improvements, informed by UI testing and direct customer feedback. 

We are in the process of overhauling key user adaptation’s UI, simplifying the overall approach while making often-used scenarios more streamlined. We’ve taken a close look at the version history (of activated adaptations) and how our users interact with it, extended the “embed third-party content” feature, widened the scope of custom fields that can be added to the UI – and more.

To ensure that we are on the right way, we need your help!

Who should participate?

Users who want to adapt standard SAPUI5 apps for business users. Both key users and technical users are welcome.

Why should you participate?

  • Gain understanding of SAPUI5 flexibility
  • Chance to influence a central SAPUI5 feature
  • Early access to prototypes

How can you participate?

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to share your feedback and engage with SAPUI5 flexibility development teams. Register by March 15, 2026, and make a true impact on how you can customize SAP apps to your needs!

Click here to sign up for our project. (A short overview about how to get started on influence.sap.com can be found here).

After you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call where you will get more information. This call is followed by individual or grouped feedback sessions.

Thank you in advance for helping us shape the future of SAPUI5 flexibility!

entry.title: Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach
entry.content:
We talk a lot about clean core.  But what happens after go-live?

Honestly, that’s where the story gets interesting.

In our webinar, “Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach,” we’ll unpack how to confidently operate extensions on SAP Business Technology Platform, even in hybrid setups working side by side with SAP S/4HANA.

Think of it like maintaining a high-performance engine.  It’s not just about adding powerful components.  It’s about tuning, monitoring, and making sure everything runs in sync.

We’ll cover account structures, multi-tenancy strategies, delivery models, and observability.  Real-world considerations.  No theory overload.  Just what you need to keep customer landscapes stable and scalable.

If you support customers day to day, this session will resonate.  And maybe even make your next project smoother.

Join us. Let’s turn clean core into a lasting operational advantage.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013041.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #CleanCoreStrategy #HybridCloud #SAPS4HANA #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio Using AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi, I'm Max McPhee, a BTP Solution Advisor at SAP. There are a lot of new capabilities coming to the SAP Business Suite, but what excites me most is that we can now build components for AI agents entirely within BTP and host those components in BTP—making it the one-stop shop for enterprise agents.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through how I built a simple MCP (Model Context Protocol) server from scratch, using AI-powered development tools, and deployed it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry for integration with Joule Studio. The best part? The entire development process was done using conversation development with GenAI tools from SAP. 

With "MCP" literally in my name, I was obligated to dive into building an MCP server. Now let's get started.

 

What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard originally invented by Anthropic that enables AI systems to interact with external tools and data sources in a standardized way. Think of it as a universal language that allows AI agents to discover and use tools without custom integration code for each one.

SAP has been an early supporter of MCP, recognizing its potential for enterprise AI scenarios. The company has released several open-source MCP servers to the community, including:

  • UI5 MCP Server - For SAPUI5 development assistance
  • CAP MCP Server - For SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • Fiori MCP Server - For SAP Fiori development

For more details on SAP's MCP, check out SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers to Enable Agentic Development.

With Joule Studio, you can connect your own custom MCP servers to extend Joule's capabilities with tools specific to your business needs.

 

Why would you want to do this?

Creating your own MCP server lets you extend Joule Studio with custom capabilities tailored to your business processes. You can connect to SAP systems, internal APIs, or any data source to give Joule new skills that understand your specific business context.

Requirements

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry account
  • Node.js installed locally
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript/Node.js
  • Access to Joule Studio with a SAP Build developer license

 

The Development Journey: AI All the Way Down

Here's where it gets meta. I used AI tools to build a tool for AI. Let me break down the process:

Step 1: Planning with Joule for Consultants

I started by asking Joule for Consultants to help me create a development plan for building an MCP server that could be deployed to SAP BTP. Joule helped me understand the architecture requirements, the SDK I'd need, and the configuration for Joule Studio integration.

 

Step 2: Building with an AI-Powered IDE

I took the plan from Joule and used it as the starting prompt in Cline, an AI coding assistant powered by models from SAP AI Core. The AI helped me:

  • Set up the Node.js project with the correct dependencies
  • Navigate the MCP SDK's API
  • Configure the streamable HTTP transport that Joule Studio requires
  • Discover the schemas necessary for proper argument handling

Step 3: Iterative Development

The AI didn't get everything right on the first try and that's okay! This is exactly why we are starting with a proof of concept scope. Through iterative debugging, we discovered important details like:

  • The MCP SDK requires a different schema than the plan expected
  • Session management is handled automatically via the mcp-session-id header
  • The transport must be "streamable HTTP" for Joule Studio compatibility

This is the beauty of AI-assisted development: rapid iteration with an intelligent partner. If I was doing this manually I would do the same thing, I would test what I built and address the errors I get in the logs, but with a coding assistant that work is done for me and I can focus on the outcome.

 

The Architecture

The end-to-end flow looks like this:

Joule for Consultants → AI-Powered IDE → MCP Server → BTP Cloud Foundry → Joule Studio

Key Components:

  • Runtime: BTP Cloud Foundry
  • MCP SDK: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  • Transport: Streamable HTTP (required for Joule Studio)
  • BTP Services: Cloud Foundry runtime, Destinations service

 

Building the MCP Server

Let me share the key pieces of the implementation.

Note: The code snippets below are excerpts to illustrate the key patterns. This blog is intended to share my experience building an MCP server on BTP, not serve as a step-by-step implementation guide.

Project Dependencies

{
  "name": "mcp-echo-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.26.0",
    "express": "^5.2.1"
  }
}

 

The Echo Tool

Here's the core pattern for registering a tool with the MCP SDK:

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { z } from "zod";

// Define the input schema using Zod
const echoInputSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string().describe("Input message to process")
});

// Register the tool
server.tool(
  "echo",
  "Returns 'bar' when input is 'foo'. Use this to test MCP connectivity.",
  echoInputSchema.shape,
  async (args, extra) => {
    const message = args.message;

    if (message === "foo") {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: "bar" }]
      };
    }

    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Echo: ${message}` }]
    };
  }
);

Key Learning: The MCP SDK requires Zod schemas for proper argument handling. Plain JSON Schema objects won't work—a detail we discovered through debugging!

 

Cloud Foundry Manifest

---
applications:
  - name: mcp-echo-server
    memory: 512M
    disk_quota: 1G
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    random-route: true
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

I've set memory to 512MB which is larger than needed for this simple echo server, but I'm using this as a MVP that I plan to expand. Starting with a reasonable production baseline avoids having to reconfigure everything later.

 

Deploying to SAP BTP

Deploying via BTP Cockpit

While you can use CF CLI I chose to do the deployment manually with the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Your Space
  2. Click Deploy Application
  3. Upload your ZIP file
  4. Select manifest.yml for configuration
  5. Deploy and wait for the application to start

 

Configuring the Destination

This is critical for Joule Studio integration. Create an HTTP destination with these settings:

Property Value

Namemcp-echo-server
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://mcp-echo-server-<random>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Propertysap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true

Important: The URL must NOT end with /mcp—Joule Studio adds that automatically! And be sure to set the additional properties so it is visible from Joule Studio.

 

Integrating with Joule Studio

With the server deployed and destination configured:

  1. Open Joule Studio
  2. Navigate to MCP Servers
  3. Click Add MCP Server
  4. Select your destination (mcp-echo-server)
  5. Configure:
    • Name: Echo Server
    • Path: /mcp (default)
  6. Save

Now test it! Ask Joule to use your echo tool by sending "foo"—you should get "bar" back.

 

One of the features that is really neat is the ability to see the execution log so you can see the steps that the agent is taking. This is a great way to help debug if you need to provide more context in your instructions. It is very important to provide the agent with the context it needs to truly be an agent and make decisions based on the tools or Joule skills it has been provided.

 

Key Learnings & Tips

Here are the most important things I learned during this project:

  1. Zod is required: The MCP SDK v1.26+ requires Zod schemas for tool input validation. Plain JSON Schema objects will fail at runtime.

  2. Transport matters: Joule Studio requires streamable HTTP transport. SSE-type destinations are not supported.

  3. URL formatting: Your destination URL must NOT end with /mcp. Joule Studio appends this automatically.

  4. Session management: The SDK handles sessions automatically via the mcp-session-id header—you don't need to implement this yourself.

  5. Structured logging: Use JSON-formatted logs for easier debugging in BTP. The Application Logging service will thank you.

 

What's Next?

This echo server is just the beginning. You can extend this pattern to:

  • Query SAP S/4HANA data and expose it as a Joule skill
  • Connect to HANA databases for real-time analytics
  • Trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows from natural language
  • Integrate with SuccessFactors for HR-related agent capabilities

 

Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues:

  • Server won't start: Check the Cloud Foundry logs in BTP Cockpit
  • Joule can't connect: Verify the destination URL doesn't end with /mcp
  • Tool not showing in Joule: Confirm the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server property is set to true
  • Schema validation errors: Ensure you're using Zod schemas, not plain JSON Schema

 

Conclusion

Using AI (from SAP AI Core) to build a tool for AI (Joule Studio), deployed on AI-ready infrastructure (BTP Cloud Foundry)—it's BTP all the way down! The barrier to entry for building MCP servers is lower than ever, especially with AI-assisted development tools at your fingertips.

 

Have you built an MCP server for Joule Studio? Please comment below if you have any questions or want to share your experience.

 

Have a great day and happy agent building!

-Max McPhee

Resources

  • SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers
  • Model Context Protocol Specification
  • Joule Studio Documentation
  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Documentation

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.

entry.title: Back2Basics Video Series - Season 5 - Hardware
entry.content:
Back2Basics videos are organized in multiple Seasons. 
Each season is about one topic around Data.
The first season was about Data and Data Management. The second season was about Data utilization. The third season was about Sustainability. The fourth season was about Artificial Intelligence.

It's now time to understand the missing part: the Hardware.
Indeed, data and hardware are very linked.
This is what we, @PatrickFavre and I, are explaining, simply so that even kids can easily understand, in this fifth season of the Back2Basics videos. 
Each video lasts less than 15 minutes, and is available in English 🇺🇸 and French 🇫🇷.

 

 

Episode 0 : Introduction

A new season to talk about the Hardware. Fundamental concepts about Computers and modern devices are presented during these 4 episodes. Each episode can be seen independently of the others, but a sequence logic is used. Episodes are released on a weekly basis, starting March 4th, 2026.

🇺🇸: https://youtu.be/pf7UU6nkM4U 
🇫🇷: https://youtu.be/KPYaPsd7fIM 

... Next update next week - Stay tuned ....

Full playlists are available here : 
🇺🇸: Playlist English
🇫🇷: Playlist French

entry.title: Implementing SAP Business Data Cloud and AI with SAP open data ecosystem
entry.content:
Ever had a customer say, “We love our SAP data… but what about everything else?”

That’s exactly where this session comes in.

We’ll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud, powered by the SAP open data ecosystem, helps you extend data boundaries and activate AI/ML across SAP and non-SAP sources.  The thing is, AI only gets exciting when it’s fed the right data and when teams actually know how to use it.

From delta share scenarios to hands-on data scientist features like notebooks, we’ll connect strategy to execution.  And yes, we’ll also give you a preview of what’s ahead on the AI roadmap.

Think of it as moving from “we store data” to “we create intelligence”.  Big difference.

Let’s build solutions that customers didn’t even realize were possible.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010668.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AIML #DigitalTransformation #DataInnovation #SAPCommunity

00;00;19;22 - 00;00;25;16
Matheus Moraes
And we had a great time there building, CAP and using AI to build applications.

00;00;25;17 - 00;00;27;25
Kevin Riedelsheimer
Cool. Vítor what about today?

00;00;27;28 - 00;00;36;27
Vítor Fiori
We are in the SAP Labs in Sao Paulo, and we had a great CodeJam learning about Joule skills and Joule agents and created in Joule Studio.

00;00;36;29 - 00;00;40;09
Kevin Riedelsheimer
And, Antonio, we're going to go to a different place tomorrow. So what's going to happen there?

00;00;40;12 - 00;00;48;07
Antonio Maradiaga
Yeah. So tomorrow we actually, doing the SAP Build event based processes. We're going to the SAP AppHouse, here in Sao Paulo.

00;00;48;09 - 00;00;51;17
Kevin Riedelsheimer
And Antonia, we have lots more coming up.

00;00;51;19 - 00;01;14;23
Antonia Rubia Zorn
Yes. So then they'll be traveling to Rio and there's more coming up in the following weeks, we’ve got Vele and Belo Horizonte. And then Tom and Rich will come to do somewhere, some more CodeJams in Sao Palo Could you, Chiba, Andrina and Nora and Vitali will also come to do some CodeJams And he. But I'll pray to you in Sao Paulo and in his CV.

00;01;14;25 - 00;01;23;27
Amanda Fragnan de Oliverira
Yeah. Gotta give us the Vampiro Brazil CodeJam roadshow.

00;01;23;29 - 00;01;30;27
Shilpa Shankar
Hello. ABAP Developers. The ABAP Cloud development model comprises of a wide range of topics that needs to be understood.

00;01;30;29 - 00;02;00;02
Shilpa Shankar
A clear guidance would be helpful to get started and to navigate ABAP cloud. If you're wondering where to find out all the relevant information, the answer is ABAP Cloud Learning Map, your guide to master ABAP cloud development model. The Learning Map gives you an overview of topics and skills along with the collection of links to the key resources, such as technology used, tools, lifecycle management, development scenarios, roadmap, best practices, and so on.

00;02;00;05 - 00;02;11;24
Shilpa Shankar
If you're a newbie or filling the knowledge gap, this learning map will help you to navigate to modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. The link to the learning map is in the description.

00;02;11;26 - 00;02;17;10
Shilpa Shankar
Check it out.

00;02;17;12 - 00;02;57;00
DJ Adams
The Developer Advocates work hard to provide learning material for you online in various forms. Deep dive videos, live streams, blog posts, challenges, tutorials and lots more. And talking of tutorials. We are revisiting something that powers much of the enterprise and that is OData. Yep. This open protocol remains essential, especially in this age of AI. What's also even more essential in this age is comprehensive understanding and in-depth knowledge.

00;02;57;03 - 00;03;23;25
DJ Adams
To that end, we're going to town on this rewrite and we'll try and make it as comprehensible and as comprehensive as we can. We're doing the rewrite in the open, as it were, so that you can see early drafts and provide feedback. We've produced six tutorials in the series so far in this rewrite, and we'd love to hear your thoughts.

00;03;23;28 - 00;03;48;04
DJ Adams
Please take a look, either in the tutorial form or in the raw document form, and send us feedback via the links you can see here. That way we can keep our content on track and produce what's most helpful to you. Thanks a lot.

00;03;48;06 - 00;04;11;14
Shilpa Shankar
Did you know you can check if your data model can access the relevant data without using an access control. In this case, I have a CDS view entity which has access control defined. Let’s check the data preview and it's limited to two records. Now click on SQL Console. To get the select statement and addition of with privileged access disabled, the access control.

00;04;11;16 - 00;04;34;26
Shilpa Shankar
Your user needs specific permission to execute ABAP source code which contains the with privileged access addition. Now this enables you to check which data a CDS entity results with or without CDS access control. In case if data is missing or only part of the expected data is displayed in the data preview, then you might not have the appropriate permissions to access the relevant data.

entry.title: Building an MCP Server to Query SAP BTP Usage Data with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Building an MCP Server to Query SAP BTP Usage Data with AI Assistants

Tags: SAP BTP, MCP, Model Context Protocol, SAP Joule, Cloud Credits, Usage API, Cloud Foundry, XSUAA, Security

Introduction

Have you ever wanted to ask your AI assistant questions like "How many cloud credits do we have left?" or "Which services are consuming the most resources?" and get instant, accurate answers based on your actual SAP BTP consumption data?

With Joule Studio + AI (to help with the MCP server) + SAP BTP Resource Consumption / Usage Data Management API (providaded by SAP), you can make it happen.

I came up with this use case because it comes up very frequently during onboarding sessions.

In this post, I walk through how I built and deployed an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server—something that has become much easier to develop today with the help of AI—to expose SAP BTP resource consumption data as tools for AI assistants and query it from SAP Joule Studio using natural language.

What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that enables AI assistants to securely access external tools and data sources. Instead of the AI having to guess or rely solely on its training data, MCP allows it to call specific tools that fetch real-time information from your systems.

Think of MCP as building blocks that extend what your AI assistant can do — in this case, querying live SAP BTP consumption data.

Architecture Overview

┌─────────────────┐     MCP (HTTP)      ┌──────────────────────┐     OAuth2      ┌──────────────────┐
│  SAP Joule      │ ──────────────────► │  MCP Server (CF App) │ ─────────────►  │  SAP BTP Usage   │
│  Studio         │   Bearer Token      │  Node.js / Express   │   via Dest.     │  API             │
└─────────────────┘                     └──────────────────────┘                 └──────────────────┘
        │                                         │
        │  OAuth2 (client_credentials)            │ validates JWT
        ▼                                         ▼
┌─────────────────┐                     ┌──────────────────────┐
│  BTP Destination│                     │  XSUAA Service       │
│  (MCP_UAS)      │                     │                      │
└─────────────────┘                     └──────────────────────┘

How it works: 1. SAP Joule Studio reads a BTP Destination with OAuth2 credentials 2. The Destination Service obtains a JWT token from XSUAA 3. Joule sends requests to the MCP server with the Bearer token 4. The MCP server validates the JWT and executes the requested tool 5. The tool fetches data from the SAP BTP Usage API via another Destination

Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have:

Requirement Details

Node.jsVersion 18 or higher
Cloud Foundry CLIcf command-line tool installed and configured
SAP BTP AccountWith access to the Usage Data Management API
PermissionsAbility to create XSUAA and Destination service instances
SAP Joule StudioEnabled in your subaccount (for the AI integration)

API Reference

The MCP server consumes the SAP BTP Resource Consumption / Usage Data Management API. You can find the full API documentation here:

🔗SAP BTP Resource Consumption API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub

This API provides endpoints for: - Cloud credits balance and contract details - Monthly and daily usage reporting - Subaccount-level consumption breakdown - Service-specific usage metrics

Security Considerations

Security is a critical aspect of this implementation. Here's how we address it:

1. Authentication with XSUAA

All MCP endpoints (/mcp and /mcp/sse) are protected by XSUAA authentication. The server:

  • Extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header
  • Validates the RS256 signature against the XSUAA public key
  • Verifies the issuer (iss) matches the configured XSUAA URL
  • Checks the audience (aud) contains the expected app name

2. OAuth2 Configuration

The xs-security.json defines three security scopes:

Scope Description

$XSAPPNAME.accessBasic access to MCP endpoints
$XSAPPNAME.readRead access to usage data
$XSAPPNAME.adminAdministrative operations

3. No Hardcoded Credentials

The server never stores API credentials in code. All sensitive data is:

  • Loaded from VCAP_SERVICES (Cloud Foundry environment)
  • Resolved at runtime via the BTP Destination Service
  • Fetched from service bindings automatically

Available MCP Tools

The server exposes six tools for querying BTP usage data:

Tool Description Example Question

sap_btp_get_cloud_creditsCloud credits balance, contract phases, expiry date"How many cloud credits do we have left?"
sap_btp_top_servicesTop N services by cost or usage"Which services are consuming the most credits?"
sap_btp_compare_monthsMonth-over-month consumption trends"Compare our consumption for the last 3 months"
sap_btp_check_overusageServices exceeding quota with PAYG costs"Which services exceeded their quota?"
sap_btp_new_servicesRecently enabled services"What new services did we enable this quarter?"
sap_btp_cost_summaryCost breakdown by service/subaccount/datacenter"Show costs grouped by subaccount"

Step-by-Step Deployment

Step 1: Clone the Repository

git clone https://github.com/sap-ef/sap-btp-usage-mcp-server.git
cd sap-btp-usage-mcp-server
npm install

🔗GitHub Repository: sap-btp-usage-mcp-server

Step 2: Create the XSUAA Service Instance

cf create-service xsuaa application sap-btp-usage-xsuaa -c xs-security.json

Create a service key to retrieve credentials:

cf create-service-key sap-btp-usage-xsuaa my-key
cf service-key sap-btp-usage-xsuaa my-key

Step 3: Create the Destination Service Instance

cf create-service destination lite sap-btp-usage-destination

Step 4: Configure the Usage API Destination

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to Connectivity > Destinations and create:

Field Value

NameSAP_BTP_USAGE_API
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://uas-reporting.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID(from your UAS service key)
Client Secret(from your UAS service key)
Token Service URLhttps://<tenant>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Note: Replace <region> with your BTP region (e.g., eu10, us10, br10).

Step 5: Build and Deploy

npm run build
cf push

Verify the deployment:

cf app sap-btp-usage-mcp-server
curl https://<your-app-url>/health
# Expected: {"status":"healthy","server":"sap-btp-usage-mcp-server"}

Connecting to SAP Joule Studio

After deploying the MCP server, you need to: 1. Create a BTP Destination so Joule can discover and authenticate against your server. 2. Create a Joule Agent in SAP Build Lobby that uses this MCP server as its toolkit.

Prerequisite: SAP Joule Studio must already be set up in your subaccount. If not, follow the Discovery Center mission first: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4643/

Step 1: Get Your Credentials

Before creating the destination, retrieve the service key credentials for your XSUAA instance:

cf service-key sap-btp-usage-xsuaa my-key

Note down the values for clientid, clientsecret, and url. You will also need the app URL from:

cf app sap-btp-usage-mcp-server | grep routes

Step 2: Create the Joule Studio Destination

In the BTP Cockpit of your Joule Studio subaccount, go to Connectivity → Destinations and create a new destination with these properties:

Field Value

NameMCP_UAS
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-app-url>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID(from service key: clientid)
Client Secret(from service key: clientsecret)
Token Service URL(from service key: url + /oauth/token)
Token Service URL TypeDedicated

Then click New Property and add:

Property Value

sap-joule-studio-mcp-servertrue

⚠️This additional property is required. Without it, Joule Studio will not discover or register your MCP server as a toolkit.

Click Save → Check Connection to verify.

Alternatively, import this JSON directly in the BTP Cockpit:

{
  "destination": {
    "Name": "MCP_UAS",
    "Type": "HTTP",
    "URL": "https://<your-app-url>",
    "ProxyType": "Internet",
    "Authentication": "OAuth2ClientCredentials",
    "clientId": "sb-sap-btp-usage-mcp-server!t<N>",
    "clientSecret": "<clientSecret from XSUAA service key>",
    "tokenServiceURL": "https://<tenant>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token",
    "tokenServiceURLType": "Dedicated",
    "sap-joule-studio-mcp-server": "true"
  }
}

Step 3: Create a Project in SAP Build Lobby

With the destination in place, open SAP Build Lobby to create the agent project:

  1. Click Create → select Joule Agent and Skill.
  2. Enter a name for the project.
  3. Click Review -> Create to confirm.

     

Step 4: Create the Agent

Inside the new project:

  1. Click Create Agent.

  2. Fill in Name and Description:

    Field Value
    NameGA_BTP_Reporting
    DescriptionAn AI-powered assistant that connects to your SAP BTP account to provide real-time insights on cloud consumption. Ask questions like: "How many cloud credits do we have left?", "Which services consumed the most this month?", "Compare our spending over the last 3 months", "Are any services exceeding their quota?"

  3. Fill in Expertise and Instructions:



    Field Value
    ExpertiseExpert on SAP BTP Global Account Reporting
    InstructionsCopy the full text from the Agent Instructions Reference.

Step 5: Add the MCP Server to the Agent

  1. Click Add MCP Server.

  2. Fill in the server details and link the destination:

    Field Value
    NameMCP_UAS
    Description for AgentYour SAP BTP FinOps assistant. Ask questions like "How many credits do we have left?", "Which services cost the most?", or "Compare January vs February spending" and get instant answers based on real-time consumption data from your Global Account.
    DestinationSelect MCP_UAS (created in Step 2)

  3. Click Add Server, then Save.

  4.  Add a calculator tool to the agent:

    • Click Add Tool.
    • Select Calculator from the list of available tools.
    • Click Add to confirm.

    This allows the agent to perform calculations when analyzing cost data.

Step 6: Test the Agent

  1. Click Test to open the test panel.

  2. Ask a sample question, for example:

    "Which 5 services consumed the most credits this month?"

If the agent returns real data from your BTP account, the integration is working correctly. 🎉

Conclusion

Building an MCP server for SAP BTP usage data opens up powerful possibilities for AI-assisted cost management and monitoring. With just a few configuration steps — deploying to Cloud Foundry, creating two BTP Destinations, and configuring a Joule Agent in SAP Build Lobby — your team can start asking natural language questions about cloud consumption and get accurate, real-time answers.

Key Takeaways

  1. MCP enables AI tools — Extend your AI assistant's capabilities with real-time data from custom sources
  2. Security first — Always protect MCP endpoints with proper authentication (XSUAA + JWT validation)
  3. No hardcoded credentials — Use BTP Destinations and service bindings for secure credential management
  4. End-to-end with Joule Studio — A BTP Destination + a Joule Agent in SAP Build Lobby is all it takes to bring your MCP server into a conversational AI experience
  5. Reusable pattern — The same approach can be applied to expose any SAP API as an MCP toolkit for Joule

Related Resources

GitHub Repository

🔗https://github.com/sap-ef/sap-btp-usage-mcp-server

SAP BTP Usage API Documentation

🔗SAP BTP Resource Consumption API – SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Model Context Protocol

🔗MCP Official Documentation

SAP Joule Studio

🔗SAP Joule Documentation 🔗 Joule Studio Setup Mission – SAP Discovery Center

entry.title: Securing Your SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2+ESM Extensions with Application Vulnerability Report-Must
entry.content:
As organizations extend SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM with custom solutions built on SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform), security becomes paramount. Whether you’re developing CAP-based services, prehooks/posthooks, custom APIs, Autoflow automations, or mashup services using JavaScript, Python, or Java—every extension must undergo rigorous security validation before reaching production environment (Extensions).

This blog explores how the Application Vulnerability Report (AVR) for CloudFoundry applications ensures your SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM extensions are production-ready and free from critical security vulnerabilities.

What is the Application Vulnerability Report (AVR)?

The AVR is SAP’s built-in security scanning tool for CloudFoundry applications deployed on BTP. It automatically analyzes your applications for:

  • Known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) in dependencies
  • Outdated libraries with security patches available
  • Configuration weaknesses in application libraries
  • Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
  • Container image vulnerabilities (for Docker-based deployments)

Key Features:

  • Automated scanning upon deployment on weekly scan
  • Detailed findings with severity ratings (Critical, High, Medium, Low)
  • Remediation guidance with version upgrade recommendations
  • Compliance alignment with industry security standards

Reference: Introducing Application Vulnerability Report for CloudFoundry Applications

Why Security Scanning Matters for SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM Side by Side Extensions

This checklist is mandatory—without any compromise—to ensure that no security vulnerabilities exist in your extensions before they are deployed to the Production environment.

SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 +ESM serves as the backbone for customer-facing operations. 

Any extensions developed on BTP—whether for data enrichment, workflow automation, or third‑party integrations—introduce potential attack vectors if not properly secured.

Additionally, many side‑by‑side extensions that are developed by your developers, who may not always account for all required security controls to ensure the application or APIs are tightly governed. In some cases, extensions may also have been built using AI-assisted development, which can inadvertently introduce vulnerabilities in libraries or application logic, including risks such as XSS, CSRF, SSRF, and others.

Common Extension Scenarios Requiring AVR:

  • CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) Services: Custom business logic exposing OData/REST APIs
  • Extensibility = External Hook (Prehooks & Posthooks) : Event-driven extensions modifying standard processes
  • Custom Microservices: Standalone services for specialized business functions
  • Autoflow Integrations: Low-code automations connecting external systems
  • Mashup Services: Composite applications combining multiple data sources

The Risk: Vulnerabilities in dependencies, libraries outdated, insecure coding practices, or misconfigurations can expose sensitive customer data or disrupt critical business processes.

Conclusion

The Application Vulnerability Report is non-negotiable for securing SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM extensions. By integrating AVR into your development lifecycle, you:

  • Protect customer data from exploits
  • Maintain compliance with security standards
  • Ensuring No security vulnerabilities violated in your extensions designed for SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM 
  • Build trust with stakeholders through proactive risk management

Call to Action for Developers and BTP Admins (Managing your BTP Landscape):

Before you move to production deployment, run AVR and clear all findings.
Your security depends on it.

entry.title: Clean Core approach with the ASAPIO Clean Core Check
entry.content:
Introduction 
In this blog, I am going to show how to use the ASAPIO Integration Add-on Clean Core Check as a proactive safeguard to ensure Clean Core compliance – without manually researching released objects, SAP Notes, or successor APIs. 

The idea behind the Clean Core Check is simple: whenever a payload or outbound object is defined, the framework validates whether the used tables or objects are released for usage in SAP S/4HANA (especially in cloud-ready or clean core scenarios). If not, it automatically suggests alternative released CDS views. 

In short: instead of discovering during an upgrade that a table like VBAK is not released, you detect it during design time – and receive a proposal such as I_SALESDOCUMENT as successor. 

This approach requires the ASAPIO Integration Add-on and is particularly relevant in S/4HANA environments where unreleased objects must be avoided. 

 

Why Clean Core matters 

SAP’s Clean Core strategy aims to: 

  • Reduce modification footprint 
  • Prevent direct access to non-released database tables 
  • Ensure upgrade stability 
  • Enforce usage of released APIs and CDS views 

Many classical SAP tables (e.g. VBAK, VBKD, VEDA) are technically available but not released for direct usage in S/4HANA Cloud or Clean Core compliant developments. 

Using them in custom extraction logic or event payloads creates technical debt and upgrade risk. 

This is exactly where the ASAPIO Clean Core Check comes into play. 

 

Example: Payload with classic SD tables 

Let’s assume we defined a Sales Order payload in the ASAPIO Payload Designer (transaction /ASADEV/DESIGN). 

We add the following tables: 

  • VBAK – Sales Document Header 
  • VBPA – Partner Data 
  • VEDA – Contract Data 
  • VBKD – Business Data 

The payload is configured and released. 

Now, instead of discovering issues during a system conversion or ATC run, we execute the Clean Core Check directly in the Payload Designer. 

 

Running the Clean Core Check 

Inside the Payload Designer, simply click on the Clean Core Check button in the toolbar. 

The framework evaluates all tables and objects used in the payload definition. 

 

 

What happens here? 

  • The system detects that VBAK, VBKD, and VEDA are not released objects 
  • It checks SAP’s released API model 
  • It proposes I_SALESDOCUMENT as an alternative CDS View 
  • It flags objects not listed in released content (e.g. VBPA) 

This happens instantly. 

 

Enabling the Clean Core Check  

Before the Clean Core Check can be executed, the corresponding repository configuration must be maintained. 

The check relies on the ASAPIO Clean Core repository, which contains the metadata about released and non-released SAP objects as well as successor CDS views. 

To activate and configure the check properly, follow the official documentation: 

https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1#use_clean_core_checks 

 

Refactoring to released CDS views 

After the Clean Core Check result, the next step is straightforward: 

  1. Remove the unreleased tables from the payload 
  1. Replace them with the suggested CDS view 
  1. Re-map the required fields 

In our example, instead of selecting from VBAK directly, we use CDS View I_SALESDOCUMENT. 

Benefits: 

  • Released API contract 
  • Semantic layer abstraction 
  • Upgrade stability 
  • Cloud readiness 
  • Compliance with Clean Core guidelines 

The payload can now be executed without violating SAP’s release strategy. 

 

Summary 

The ASAPIO Clean Core Check provides: 

  • Immediate detection of non-released tables 
  • Automatic suggestion of released CDS alternatives 
  • Support for SAP Clean Core strategy 
  • Reduced upgrade risks 
  • Faster development cycles 

Instead of reacting to ATC findings during system conversion, developers can proactively design compliant payloads from the beginning. 

It shifts Clean Core validation directly into the design phase. 

In other words: 

Configuration-driven integration, now also Clean Core compliant. 

Let me know if you would like to see a deeper technical walkthrough or how the check is implemented internally in the Add-on.

entry.title: How to use BPA API Trigger using Fiori
entry.content:
Introduction: How to make your Fiori app talk to SAP Build Process Automation (BPA)? Well, I'm going to show you exactly how to trigger BPA workflows directly from your Fiori application using API triggers.
Think of it like this: your user fills out a form in Fiori, hits submit, this enables communication between Fiori and BPA, allowing your workflow to start automatically based on form input.
 
What we're building today:
  1. A Fiori form that captures sales order data
  2. A BPA workflow that gets triggered automatically
  3. The connection between them using API triggers

An API trigger - It is a BPA allows external systems such as SAP Fiori, CPI, or any REST capable application to start a process by calling an API endpoint.

Part 1: Creating the BPA Automation with API Trigger

1. Go to BTP Lobby -> Create - > Automated Process -> Process.
You’ll see a new process canvas with an option to add a trigger.

2. Click API Trigger to set this as the starting point of your workflow.

3. Give a suitable name and click Create.

4. Add a Data Type that contains the fields you want to receive from Fiori.

5. Create a custom variable using the same Data Type created above.

6. Your workflow might look like this:

API Trigger
Condition (e.g., check if Net Amount > 1000)
-> If Yes ->  Auto Approval
-> If No -> User Approval
->  Acknowledgement Form or Rejection Form

Once ready, Save → Release → Deploy the process.

Note: After deployment only it will appear in Environment 

7. Go to Control Tower -> Environments -> Public (or your selected environment).
Click the three dots -> View.

8. Below is the sample JSON payload which it used to map values 

Copy this  you’ll use it in your Fiori app.

Part 2: Creating the Fiori App (Freestyle SAPUI5)

1. Open BAS -> File -> New Project from Template.

2. Select SAP Fiori generator and click start.

3. Select basic to create freestyle application and click next.

 

4. select Data Source and Service Selection

  • Data Source: Connect to a system

  • System: Choose your SAP system

  • Service: Select the OData service

Click Next.

5. Select yes for deployment configuration and click next.

6. Deployment configuration

  • Target -> Cloud Foundry

  • Destination Name -> Your destination

  • Add Router Module -> Managed Application Router

Finish the wizard.

7. In Cloud Foundry sign in and targets Choose SSO Passcode.
Click the link ->  Select Identity Provider -> Enter Passcode -> Sign in.

Click the URL, and it will take you to the Identity Provider selection page. Choose the default provider, verify that your user ID is correct, and continue. A passcode will be generated copy it, paste it and sign in.

8. simple Fiori form 

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="salesorder.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout"
    xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form">

    <Page id="page" title="Sales Order Form">

        <VBox id="_IDGenVBox1" class="sapUiSmallMargin"   alignItems="Center">

            <!-- Centered container with limited width -->
            <VBox id="_IDGenVBox2" width="50%" class="sapUiSmallMarginAuto"  >

                <f:SimpleForm id="_IDGenSimpleForm"
                    editable="true"
                    layout="ResponsiveGridLayout"
                    labelSpanL="4" labelSpanM="4"
                    adjustLabelSpan="false"
                    emptySpanL="1" emptySpanM="1"
                    columnsL="1" columnsM="1">

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel" text="Sales Order"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrder}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel1" text="Sales Org"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput1" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrg}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel2" text="Distribution Channel"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput2" value="{salesorderdetails/DistributionChannel}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel3" text="Division"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput3" value="{salesorderdetails/Division}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel4" text="Document Date"/>
                    <DatePicker id="_IDGenDatePicker" value="{salesorderdetails/DocumentDate}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel5" text="Created By"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput4" value="{salesorderdetails/CreatedByUser}"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel6" text="Customer Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput5" value="{salesorderdetails/CustomerNumber}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel7" text="Order Amount"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput6" value="{salesorderdetails/OrderAmount}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel8" text="Purchase Order Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput7" value="{salesorderdetails/PurchaseOrderNumber}" required="true"/>

                </f:SimpleForm>

                <!-- Submit button aligned to right -->
                <HBox id="_IDGenHBox" justifyContent="End" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop">
                    <Button id="_IDGenButton" text="Submit" type="Emphasized" press=".onSubmit"/>
                </HBox>

            </VBox>

        </VBox>

    </Page>

</mvc:View>

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("salesorder.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        
         _getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
            var oComponent = sap.ui.core.Component.getOwnerComponentFor(this.getView());
                if (oComponent) {
                    var sAppId = oComponent.getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
                    var sAppPath = sAppId.replaceAll(".", "/");
                    var sAppModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(sAppPath);
                    return sAppModulePath;
                } else {
                    console.error("Component could not be found.");
                    return "";
                }
    
        },
        onSubmit() {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var url = "/sap_process_automation_service/workflow/rest/v1/workflow-instances";
            var sUrl = this._getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL() + url;
            try {
                var payload = {
                    "definitionId": "eu10.fin-dev-project.salesorder522.salesOrder",
                        "context": {
                        "salesorderdetails": {
                            "SalesOrder":  oView.byId("_IDGenInput").getValue(),
                                "SalesOrg": oView.byId("_IDGenInput1").getValue(),
                                    "DistributionChannel": oView.byId("_IDGenInput3").getValue(),
                                        "Division": oView.byId("_IDGenInput2").getValue(),
                                            "DocumentDate": String(oView.byId("_IDGenDatePicker").getValue() || ""),
                                                "CreatedByUser": oView.byId("_IDGenInput4").getValue(),
                                                    "CustomerNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput5").getValue()||""),
                                                        "OrderAmount": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput6").getValue() || ""),
                                                            "PurchaseOrderNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput7").getValue() || "")
                        }
                    }
                }
                console.log("Payload: ", payload);
                  $.ajax({
                url: sUrl,
                type: "POST",
                dataType: "json",
                contentType: "application/json",
                data: JSON.stringify(payload),
                beforeSend: function (xhr) {
                    xhr.setRequestHeader("Accept", "application/json");
                },
                success: function (response) {
                    console.log("Success: ", response);
                }.bind(this),
                error: function (error) {
                    console.log("Error in POST call:", error);
                }

            });

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("Submission failed:", error);
            }
        }
    });
});

xs.app.json -

//add this in routes   
   {
      "source": "^/process_automation_service/(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "destination": "process_automation_service",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa",
      "csrfProtection": false
    }

9. Right-click mta.yaml -> Build MTA Project.
This generates an .mtar file in mta_archives.

10. Right-click the .mtar file -> Deploy MTA Archive.

11. Go to BTP cockpit -> HTML5 Applications -> click your app.

12. Fill the form -> Click Submit.
you will get success message in console

If it fails, check:

  • Destination configuration

  • xs-app.json routes

  • BPA credentials

13. Once triggered, the workflow reaches the approver in the BPA Inbox.

14. you will receive the acknowledge form once you received you can accept it.

15. Once everything is over you will see the completed status

16 Click the instance to see:

  • step details

  • submitted data

  • log

Note: In BPA, "Started By" usually shows the SAP user.
But when triggering through external systems (like Fiori), it may generate a system string instead of the real user ID.

So design your workflow logic accordingly.

Conclusion:  You've successfully built a seamless integration between SAP Fiori and SAP Build Process Automation.

What you accomplished:

  • Created an end-to-end automated workflow from Fiori form submission to BPA approval process
  • Implemented real-time API communication using REST triggers
  • Established a reusable integration pattern for future business processes

You're combining the user-friendly interface of Fiori with the powerful automation capabilities of BPA, while maintaining full connectivity to your core ABAP systems - creating a comprehensive end-to-end solution.

entry.title: My SAP Business AI Certification Journey
entry.content:
When I started these SAP Business AI courses (Discovering SAP Business AI, Positioning SAP Business AI and Boosting AI-driven Business Transformation with Joule Agents), I expected a standard product overview. What I got instead was a completely new way of thinking about enterprise technology, AI strategy, and how to have meaningful conversations with clients about digital transformation.

This blog is my attempt to share what I learned, how I passed my certification, and what surprised me along the way — for anyone about to take the same journey.

SAP Certification Name: SAP Certified - Positioning SAP Business AI Solutions as part of SAP Business Suite (C_BCBAI)

1. What the Certification Covers

The certification spans three SAP Learning courses, each building on the previous one. Together they cover the full SAP Business AI story — from strategy to technical architecture to real-world deployment.

Course

Focus

Key Learning

Discovering SAP Business AI

Why AI? What is SAP's strategy?

The SAP Flywheel — how Applications, Data and AI reinforce each other

Positioning SAP Business AI

How to position AI to different personas

Joule, AI Foundation, Responsible AI, Sustainability — and how each lands with CIO, CFO, COO

Joule Agents & Transformation

How AI agents actually work in business

Agentic AI, Business Data Cloud, Knowledge Graph, AI Agent Hub — the full technical picture

2. The Three Things That Changed How I Think

A. AI Without Data Is Just Noise

The biggest insight from Course 1 was simple but profound: AI is only as good as the data feeding it. Before these courses, I used to talk about AI features. Now I start every conversation with the data foundation.

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is what makes AI reliable — connecting all systems using zero-copy federation, preserving business context through the Semantic Layer, and keeping data sovereign in each region. Without BDC, AI outputs are guesswork.

B. Joule Is Not Just a Chatbot

Before the certification, I described Joule as "SAP's AI assistant." After it, I describe it very differently. Joule is an omnipresent AI Copilot with 1,800+ skills, covering 80% of SAP's most-used transactions, available in 11 languages, and capable of proactively acting before users even ask.

And then there are Joule Agents — a completely different level. Agents reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows autonomously. The Cash Management Agent saves finance teams up to 70% of manual reconciliation time. The Production Planning Agent autonomously checks material and capacity. 

C. Responsible AI Is a Commercial Accelerator

I used to treat ethics and compliance as a defensive topic — something to address when clients raised concerns. The certification changed that. SAP's Responsible AI framework is actually a competitive advantage.

  • Client data never leaves SAP's governed architecture — never used to train third-party models
  • Every Joule action is fully auditable — no black-box decisions
  • Data sovereignty built in — GDPR in Europe, PDPA in Asia, automatic
  • ISO 42001 certified for AI governance — internationally recognised standard
  • Human-in-the-loop by design — AI recommends, humans approve critical decisions.

3. How I Applied the Learning in the Certification

The certification assessment is scenario-based — which I found genuinely challenging and genuinely useful. Here is what helped me prepare and what I would tell anyone taking it next.

Scenario Type

What It Tests

My Approach

Client objection handling

Can you position BDC and Joule against a "we don't need to change" mindset?

Memorised the control tower analogy — ERPs are engines, BDC is the control tower. Never replace, always layer.

Persona-based positioning

Which message lands with which persona — CIO vs CFO vs COO?

Built a mental matrix: CIO = integration + security, CFO = ROI + reconciliation time, COO = supply chain speed

Technical architecture

How does zero-copy federation work? What is the Knowledge Graph?

Used real examples: Knowledge Graph maps PO to Supplier to Invoice — grounding AI in business context, preventing hallucinations

Responsible AI scenarios

What happens to client data? How is compliance maintained?

Led with ISO 42001, data sovereignty, human-in-the-loop — not features, but governance principles

The exam rewards consultative thinking, not product recall. The question is rarely "what does Joule do?" It is almost always "given this client situation, what would you recommend and why?" Thinking like a consultant — not a salesperson — is the key.

4. What I Gained Beyond the Certificate

Certifications give you a credential. Good certifications give you a new lens. This one gave me both.

  • Confidence in client conversations — I can now discuss AI architecture, data sovereignty, and agent capabilities without hesitation or vagueness.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.
  • A structured positioning framework — I know which message to lead with for each persona and which technical detail to deploy for each objection.
  • Real examples that land — not "SAP has AI agents" but "the Cash Management Agent saves your finance team 70% of manual reconciliation time — here is exactly how."
  • A sustainability angle I was not expecting — the Green Ledger and Sustainability Tower capabilities genuinely surprised me with their depth and maturity.
  • A new appreciation for data — the courses reinforced that AI is a data story first and a technology story second. That reframing has changed how I approach every client engagement.

The most valuable moment in the entire certification was a scenario-based roleplay where I had to respond to a sceptical client who said "our ERP works fine, why change it?" Every concept from all three courses came together in that one moment. That is when I knew the learning had actually landed.

Final Thought

If you are considering this certification — do it. Not just for the badge, but for the way it reshapes how you think about AI, data, and enterprise transformation.

And when you sit down with your next client who says "we are curious about AI but not sure where to start" — you will know exactly what to say.

Start with the data. Layer the intelligence. Let the Flywheel turn.

If this was useful, please like and share. Questions or your own experiences? Drop them in the comments below. Happy to connect with fellow SAP learners.
